beats.prompt: Before we start — what is a 0/1 indicator variable?

interaction.title: The indicator: a 0/1 switch
interaction.body: An **indicator variable** 1_A equals **1** when event A happens, and **0** when it doesn't. Nothing more — it's a switch. Its expected value is exactly the probability the switch turns on: E[1_A] = P(A).

feedback.correct: Good — a 0/1 switch whose mean is exactly P(A). Let's recall some probabilities you already computed.
hints: An indicator is 1 when the event fires, 0 otherwise.
Its expected value is its probability of being 1.
Continue.

beats.prompt: You've computed these probabilities before. Match each situation to its value — they're each secretly E[1_A] for the right indicator.

pairs.left: P(win) in Gambler's Ruin, starting at i chips of N
P(winning the streak bet, PHT lesson)

feedback.correct: Each value is P(A) — and now you know that's E[1_A] too. Probability and expectation collapse into one for a 0/1 variable.
hints: Tap a probability description, then its formula.
Gambler's Ruin P(win | start i, goal N) = i/N.
Match all three.

beats.prompt: Shuffle a 52-card deck and flip one card at a time. How deep do you expect to dig before the first ace turns up — about halfway?

Around card 26.note: Let's test it — 'halfway' assumes one ace, but four aces split the deck, pulling the first one much earlier.

Around card 13.note: Let's test it — warmer; four aces make five equal gaps, so the first ace averages near card 11.

Around card 10.note: Good instinct — let's prove it: five equal gaps from four aces put the first ace near 10.6.

beats.prompt: Draw one card at random from a shuffled 52-card deck. Let 1_ace = 1 if it's an ace, 0 otherwise. What is E[1_ace]?

feedback.correct: E[1_ace] = P(ace) = 4/52 = 1/13. A 0/1 variable needs no long sum — its mean is just P.
hints: A 0/1 variable needs no long sum. Its mean is just P(ace) = 4/52 = 1/13.
There are 4 aces in 52 cards. P(ace) = 4/52.

beats.prompt: Draw cards one at a time. Each draw records a 1 (ace) or 0 (not ace). Watch the running average settle.

interaction.gamblerNote: As draws grow, the average of 0s and 1s settles to P(ace) = 1/13 ≈ 0.077 — that is E[1_ace].

hero.structuralReadout: Running average settles to P(ace) = 1/13 as draws grow.

feedback.correct: The average of 0s and 1s is the hit-rate — and the hit-rate is P(ace). That's E[1_A]=P(A) in motion.
hints: Run a large batch and watch the average stabilize.
The running average of 0s and 1s equals the fraction of aces drawn.
That fraction converges to P(ace) = 1/13.

beats.prompt: Tap the formula terms to see why E[1_A]=P(A) — and how any count becomes a sum of probabilities.

interaction.title: The indicator trick
interaction.body:
**E[1_A] = 1·P(A) + 0·P(Aᶜ) = P(A).** The only nonzero value is 1, taken with probability P(A).

Any count of events is a sum of 0/1 indicators: **count = Σ 1_{A_i}**. By linearity (from L2, no independence needed): **E[count] = Σ E[1_{A_i}] = Σ P(A_i)**. A messy expectation collapses to a sum of plain probabilities you already know how to compute.

feedback.correct: E[1_A]=P(A) — probability and expectation are one and the same for a 0/1 indicator. A count is a sum of indicators, so E[count]=ΣP(A_i) by linearity.
hints: Any count = Σ indicators; linearity (L2) adds their expectations without needing independence.
Continue.

beats.prompt: Before the mastery challenge: a closer look at the five-region argument.

interaction.title: Five equal gaps
interaction.body:
Place 4 aces randomly among 52 cards. They divide the deck into **5 regions**: before ace 1, between aces 1–2, 2–3, 3–4, and after ace 4.

Each of the 48 non-aces is equally likely to land in any of these 5 positions relative to the 4 aces. So **P(non-ace i precedes all 4 aces) = 1/5**.

Let I_i = 1 if non-ace i comes before all aces. E[I_i] = 1/5 for each i. The expected position of the first ace = 1 (for the ace itself) + Σ E[I_i] over all 48 non-aces.

feedback.correct: The key: 4 aces create 5 equal slots. Each non-ace lands in any slot with probability 1/5.
hints: 4 aces → 5 regions in any deck order.
Each non-ace has P=1/5 of being positioned before all aces.
Continue.

beats.prompt: Roll a fair 6-sided die twice (N=6 faces, m=2 rolls). Let I_i = 1 if face i appears at least once. What is the expected number of distinct faces seen?

feedback.correct: E[distinct] = 6(1−(5/6)²) = 6·11/36 = 11/6 ≈ 1.83. The two rolls can land on the same face, so it's below 2.
hints: The two rolls might repeat a face. Sum each face's chance of appearing: 6(1−(5/6)²) = 11/6.
E[I_i] = 1 − P(face i never appears in 2 rolls) = 1 − (5/6)².

beats.prompt: Mastery challenge: use indicator variables to find the expected number of cards turned over before (and including) the first ace.

interaction.scenario: Four aces divide the 52 cards into 5 equally likely positions for each non-ace. Let \(I_i = 1\) if non-ace \(i\) precedes all 4 aces. There are 48 non-aces. What is \(E[\text{first ace position}]\)?

beats.interviewNote: The 5-equal-gaps argument (Green Book p.48): four aces create five 'slots' in any random ordering of 52 cards. By symmetry, each of the 48 non-aces lands in any of the five slots with equal probability 1/5. So P(non-ace i precedes all aces) = 1/5. Therefore E[first ace] = 1 + Σ_{i=1}^{48} E[I_i] = 1 + 48·(1/5) = 53/5 ≈ 10.6. Clean interview answer: no casework, no recursion, just one symmetry observation.

feedback.correct: Let \( I_i = 1 \) if non-ace \( i \) appears before all four aces. The four aces create five equally likely gaps for each non-ace, so: \[ E[\text{first ace}] = 1 + \sum_{i=1}^{48} P(I_i = 1) \] With \( P(I_i=1) = \tfrac{1}{5} \) by symmetry: \( 1 + 48 \cdot \tfrac{1}{5} = \tfrac{5}{5}+\tfrac{48}{5} = \tfrac{53}{5} \approx 10.6 \). Four aces create five equal gaps — one symmetry observation replaces all casework.
hints: Each non-ace adds only its \(\tfrac{1}{5}\) chance of preceding every ace. Sum: \(1 + 48 \cdot \tfrac{1}{5} = \tfrac{53}{5}\).
Let \(I_i = 1\) if non-ace \(i\) precedes all 4 aces. By symmetry (5 equal gaps), \(P(I_i=1)=\tfrac{1}{5}\). Then \(E = 1 + 48 \cdot \tfrac{1}{5}\).

beats.prompt: Reveal the recap cards — the indicator bridge in three moves.

feedback.correct: Indicators: the bridge from probability to expectation. A count of events = a sum of probabilities you already know. Sets up L5 — a full coupon set as a sum of geometric waits.
hints: E[count]=ΣP(A_i) by linearity, no independence needed.
L5 next: coupon collector via the same trick.
